question: [File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: linkNextHeaderValue()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a request that causes `linksNext` (from `res.locals.responseData.links?.next`) to contain a URL pointing to a different timestamp range, so that a client following the `Link` header skips or replays a segment of Hashgraph transaction history, causing an incomplete or falsified view of the ledger?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a crafted `originalUrl` containing newline characters (`%0a`) so that `logger.info(\`${req.ip} ${req.method} ${req.originalUrl} in ${elapsed} ms: ${code}\`)` emits a forged log line that falsely records a different transaction ID or consensus timestamp as having been served, corrupting the audit trail of Hashgraph history access?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the spread merge `{...headers.default, ...(headers.path[path] ?? {}), ...(res.locals[responseHeadersLabel] ?? {})}` by triggering a route handler that writes attacker-controlled data into `res.locals[responseHeadersLabel]`, overriding `cache-control` to `max-age=0` so that a legitimately cached Hashgraph history response is evicted and replaced with a stale or tampered version?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user send a request where `res.locals[requestPathLabel]` is set to a path that matches a different `headers.path` entry (e.g., a path with a longer `max-age`), causing the response for a mutable Hashgraph history endpoint to be cached for an extended period and served stale to subsequent users, effectively hiding recent ledger changes?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that `res.locals.responseData.links?.next` is read directly from `res.locals` (line 42) without sanitization, and if a route handler populates `links.next` with an absolute URL to an attacker-controlled server, cause API clients to follow the `Link` header to a server that returns falsified Hashgraph transaction history?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: linkNextHeaderValue()] Can an unprivileged external user trigger a code path where `linksNext` contains a crafted string with `>; rel=

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `JSONStringify` (json-bigint) serialization of `responseData` to cause precision loss on large BigInt transaction amounts (e.g., HBAR balances exceeding `Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER`), resulting in the REST API returning numerically incorrect transfer amounts that misrepresent the true Hashgraph ledger state?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user send a request to a valid route that returns `responseData` with a `links.next` value containing a timestamp parameter set far in the past (e.g., `timestamp=lt:0`), causing pagination clients to loop indefinitely or skip the entire transaction history window, effectively denying access to Hashgraph history?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `code >= 400 || Math.random() <= sampleRate` logging condition (line 56) by crafting requests that return 2xx responses, relying on the probabilistic `sampleRate` to suppress access logs, thereby hiding repeated probing of Hashgraph history endpoints from audit logs?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that `res.locals[responseBodyLabel]` is set to the raw `responseData` object (not JSON-serialized) when `contentType !== APPLICATION_JSON`, and if a route handler sets a non-JSON content type via `responseHeadersLabel`, cause binary or raw data to be sent that bypasses JSON integrity checks on Hashgraph history records?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the header merge order `{...headers.default, ...(headers.path[path] ?? {}), ...(res.locals[responseHeadersLabel] ?? {})}` to inject a `content-type` header that differs from `APPLICATION_JSON`, bypassing the `JSONStringify` serialization branch and causing raw JavaScript objects (with prototype properties) to be sent as the response body, potentially leaking internal Hashgraph state?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user send a request where `req.route.path` is undefined (e.g., via a middleware-only route), causing `res.locals[requestPathLabel] ?? req.route.path` to throw a TypeError that bypasses normal response handling and leaves the Hashgraph history response in an inconsistent state?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `res.locals.statusCode` value (set to 200 by default in `requestLogger`) by triggering a route handler that sets `statusCode` to `206` (PARTIAL_CONTENT) for a complete Hashgraph history response, causing downstream caching layers to treat the full history as partial and serve incomplete data to other users?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a request to a transaction history endpoint where the `links.next` URL contains a `timestamp` parameter that overlaps with the current page's range, causing pagination clients to receive duplicate Hashgraph transaction records and miscount the total ledger history?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `JSONStringify` (json-bigint) serialization to produce JSON with duplicate keys (e.g., if `responseData` contains prototype-polluted properties), causing different JSON parsers used by API clients to interpret Hashgraph transaction fields differently, leading to inconsistent views of ledger history?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `res.locals[responseHeadersLabel]` spread to inject a `cache-control: no-store` header for a Hashgraph history endpoint, preventing legitimate caching and forcing every client to re-query the database, enabling a timing-based attack to observe when new transactions are added to the ledger?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: linkNextHeaderValue()] Can an unprivileged external user trigger a response where `linksNext` is set to a URL containing a `consensus_timestamp` that is one nanosecond before the first record in the current page, causing pagination clients to re-fetch the same Hashgraph history page in an infinite loop and never advance through the ledger?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that `res.locals.responseData` is accessed directly on line 42 (`res.locals.responseData.links?.next`) while `responseData` variable is set on line 27 (`res.locals[responseDataLabel]`), and if `responseDataLabel` constant differs from the string `'responseData'`, cause a discrepancy where the serialized body and the `Link` header point to different data, corrupting Hashgraph history pagination?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user send a request with a crafted `X-Forwarded-For` header that overrides `req.ip` in the log line `logger.info(\`${req.ip} ...\`)`, causing the access log to attribute Hashgraph history queries to a different IP address and defeating IP-based audit correlation?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `headers.path[path]` lookup where `path` is derived from `res.locals[requestPathLabel] ?? req.route.path`, and by manipulating the path to match a route with a very long `max-age` cache header, cause a Hashgraph history response to be cached for an extended period, hiding subsequent ledger updates from API consumers?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `JSONStringify` serialization of `responseData` containing a `transaction_hash` field with a crafted value that is valid JSON but contains Unicode escape sequences, causing different clients to decode the hash differently and fail to verify the integrity of Hashgraph transaction records?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `res.locals[responseHeadersLabel]` override to inject an `etag` header with a crafted value, causing the `responseCacheCheckHandler` to serve a cached Hashgraph history response to a client that sends a matching `if-none-match` header, even though the underlying ledger data has changed?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user send a request to a Hashgraph history endpoint with a `limit` parameter set to 0 or a negative value, causing `links.next` to be populated with a URL that skips all records, and `responseHandler` to serialize and send an empty transaction list as if it were the complete history?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `res.locals[responseBodyLabel] = contentType === APPLICATION_JSON ? JSONStringify(responseData) : responseData` branch by triggering a route that sets `responseHeadersLabel` to override `content-type` to a non-JSON value, causing the raw `responseData` object reference to be stored in `responseBodyLabel` and subsequently cached by `responseCacheUpdateHandler` as a non-serialized object, corrupting the Redis cache with invalid Hashgraph history data?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `Math.random() <= sampleRate` condition (default `sampleRate=0.1`) to statistically suppress 90% of access logs for Hashgraph history endpoints, enabling bulk enumeration of transaction history without triggering rate-limit or anomaly detection systems that rely on log volume?
